fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large Cap Fund (erstwhile Bluechip Fund) - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Core equity holding — low-cost (0.20% ER), large-cap blend of Nifty 50 stocks, historically the most reliable long-run performer. Large-cap funds have lower volatility than mid/small caps, which suits Phoebe's moderate risk profile. At 28 with 28 years ahead, this is the biggest bucket. ₹60,000 lumpsum from existing corpus + ₹7,200/month SIP.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Aggressive Hybrid fund — automatically holds ~65% stocks + ~35% bonds within a single fund. This means built-in diversification and automatic debt exposure without the need for a separate debt fund. Low beta (0.57) means it falls less sharply than the market. At 28 years, this serves as the 'debt + stability' portion of the portfolio in a single fund wrapper. ₹30,000 lumpsum + ₹3,600/month SIP.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: 10% allocation to a passive S&P 500 index fund gives Phoebe broad exposure to the US stock market — a low-cost way to diversify beyond Indian markets. At 0.20% ER, this is one of the cheapest ways to gain global exposure. Beta of just 0.04 means it moves almost completely independently from Indian markets, which is a genuine diversifier. ₹10,000 lumpsum + ₹1,200/month SIP.

plan.setup_phase: MONTH 1 — Set up 3 SIP mandates on the 5th of every month (before salary processing). Set up direct plan versions of all three funds (avoid regular plans with commission-bearing distributors). Set up a separate savings account or sweep-in account for the ₹100,000 existing corpus — do NOT invest the emergency fund money first. Keep 3 months of expenses in a FD or savings account as your emergency fund target. Once that is done (typically 1-2 months), deploy the remaining corpus into the 3 mutual funds via lumpsum.
review_checkpoints: March 2026 (Year 1): Confirm all 3 SIPs running, emergency fund target met, check that ₹60,000 + ₹30,000 + ₹10,000 lumpsums have been invested
March 2027 (Year 2): First rebalancing — check if any fund has drifted more than 5% from allocation, rebalance if needed
March 2030 (Year 5): Marriage/travel goal review — check corpus value vs target, decide if you need to redirect funds or step up SIP
March 2035 (Year 10): Mid-term review — assess if risk profile still moderate, rebalance if needed
March 2043 (Year 20): Major life review — reassess goals, income, risk profile, consider shifting to conservative allocation
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Check your portfolio ONCE a year — ideally after March 31 when annual returns are visible. If any fund has drifted more than 5% from its target allocation (e.g., S&P 500 grows too fast and takes 15% of portfolio), REDIRECT new SIPs to the lagging funds first. Do NOT sell and rebuy — just adjust your future SIP amounts. Only sell and rebuy if the drift is 10%+ and has persisted for 2+ years. Never rebalance more than once a year. Never rebalance in response to market news or fund manager commentary.
plan.rationale: Phoebe is 27, has a 28-year horizon, moderate risk appetite, and a variable income. The plan is built around 3 simple, passive, low-cost funds (total expense ratio ~0.20% across all three) — no active managers, no sector bets, no complex multi-asset mandates. The 60-30-10 split is broad and defensible: Large Cap (India) + Aggressive Hybrid (India) + International Index. This provides domestic market exposure, built-in debt, and a genuine global diversifier. The lumpsum deployment of ₹1,00,000 from existing corpus is split across the 3 funds proportionally. The SIP step-up is set at 10% annually — just the rate of average salary growth for a UX designer in Pune — ensuring the plan scales naturally. Because Phoebe's income varies and she cannot commit a fixed SIP every month, the plan has no minimum SIP requirement — her ₹12,000/month is just the ideal target, not a rigid mandate. If a month comes where she can only invest ₹6,000, she can switch to — say — a lumpsum top-up in the S&P 500 fund instead. Flexibility is built into the plan.
risks: Market risk: Indian and US equity markets can fall 30-50% in bad years — this is normal and temporary
Currency risk: S&P 500 fund returns are in USD, so a strong INR can reduce returns
Inflation risk: Over 28 years, inflation could erode real returns — equity-heavy portfolios historically outpace inflation
Income volatility: Since income varies, SIPs may pause or reduce — this is okay, just restart when possible
Concentration risk: All three funds are from ICICI Pru — if anything happens to the fund house, all 3 funds could be affected together
Tax risk: New regime tax slabs mean lower-taxed gains — keep this in mind for redemption timing
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase total monthly SIP by 10% every year — roughly matching the average salary growth trajectory of a UX designer in Pune. If one month's salary is low, just pause SIPs and top up later — the step-up is a goal, not a rule.

allocation_schedule.trigger: Start — 28-year horizon, aggressive allocation as equity growth is the only reliable wealth-builder at this age
Marriage/travel goal review — begin gradual shift to more stability, add 5% gold if gold prices are attractive
Mid-career review — shift to 70-25-5 as income stabilises and goals near
Towards retirement — begin decumulation planning, redirect to SWP or annuity options

plan.perspective: Phoebe, you're 27, on a 28-year investment journey — that's the biggest advantage anyone can have. At this age, market volatility is your friend, not your enemy. When you're 55, a 20% market drop will feel like a minor speed bump, not a catastrophe. The plan is designed to be as simple as possible: 3 funds, 10% annual SIP step-up, and a review once a year. If a month comes where you can't invest the full ₹12,000, that's okay — just top up when you can next. Your existing ₹1,00,000 corpus is deployed as a lumpsum across all three funds. The S&P 500 slice is your global diversifier — it moves almost independently from Indian markets, which is exactly the point.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: Only 10% allocation is to passive instruments (S&P 500 index fund), while 90% equity allocation is actively managed funds (large cap and hybrid).
turnover_score: Annual rebalancing with minimal trading (only if drift >5-10% and only through SIP redirection, not selling).
cost_emphasis_score: Strong focus on low costs (0.20% ER across all funds) and avoiding commission-based plans.
research_vs_cost_score: Emphasizes broad market exposure and diversification rather than individual stock research.
time_horizon_alignment_score: 28-year horizon with infrequent reviews and long-term compounding focus.
portfolio_activeness_score: Mixed - while the large cap fund has active characteristics (tracking error 7.91%, alpha 12.54), the hybrid fund provides built-in diversification and the S&P 500 is purely passive. Overall portfolio shows moderate activeness.

pqs.reasoning:
Goal Alignment (0.85): The plan aligns well with Phoebe's goals - large cap for long-term wealth, hybrid fund for stability, and international exposure for diversification. It addresses her need for flexibility with variable income and simple portfolio structure. However, it doesn't specifically address the 5-year marriage/travel fund goal with a separate allocation or strategy.

Diversification (0.7): Good diversification across asset classes (equity, hybrid, international) and geographies. However, there's significant concentration risk with 90% of funds from ICICI Prudential (large cap and hybrid), which is noted as a risk but not mitigated. The international exposure is good but limited to 10%.

Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.8): The moderate risk profile matches Phoebe's stated appetite. Projections seem reasonable for a 28-year horizon. The plan acknowledges market risks appropriately and provides guidance on handling income volatility.

Internal Consistency (0.9): Very consistent - rationale, allocations, rebalancing guidelines, and risk warnings all tell a coherent story. The plan acknowledges fund house concentration risk and provides clear mitigation strategies.

Tax Efficiency (0.7): Good tax optimization for new_regime investor - avoids ELSS appropriately, uses equity-taxed funds (large cap and aggressive hybrid). However, doesn't explicitly discuss the tax advantages of the hybrid fund structure or mention tax-efficient rebalancing strategies.
